title: PEFT
lesson:
PEFT stands for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning. It is two things at once, and people sloppily use the word for both. First, it is the idea: instead of retraining all the numbers inside a model, you freeze the giant model and train only a tiny slice of new numbers. Second, it is a specific software library from Hugging Face, literally called peft, that implements that idea for you in a few lines of Python. This lesson covers both, because to use the library well you need the idea behind it.

The problem, in plain numbers

A model's skill lives in its parameters — the billions of learned numbers it multiplies your text against. "Full" fine-tuning nudges every one of those numbers. To do that, the GPU must hold the whole model plus a pile of training bookkeeping (gradients and optimiser state — roughly the model's size several times over). That is why fully fine-tuning even a 7-billion-parameter model can demand 60GB+ of VRAM (the memory on your graphics card). PEFT cuts that down to something a single consumer GPU can handle, by training maybe 0.1% of the numbers and leaving the other 99.9% frozen.

Analogy 1: editing a movie

Imagine a finished 3-hour film already shot, scored, and colour-graded (the frozen base model). A studio note says "make it feel warmer and tighten the intro." You do not reshoot the movie. You add a colour-grade layer and a few trims on top. The original footage is untouched; your small edit-list rides above it and you can revert in one click. PEFT is that edit-list — a tiny, removable layer that steers a huge thing you never re-create.

Analogy 2: a translator's cheat-sheet

A fluent translator (the frozen model) already knows the language. To work at your company, they do not relearn the language — they tape a one-page cheat-sheet of your jargon and house style to their desk. Tiny effort, big behaviour change. Swap clients, swap the cheat-sheet. PEFT trains that cheat-sheet, not the translator.

PEFT is a family, not one trick

PEFT is an umbrella over several methods. They differ in where they bolt on the small trainable bit, but all share the same deal: freeze the big thing, train the small thing.

  • The headliner is LoRA, which adds two skinny matrices beside existing weights.
  • Its memory-saving cousin is QLoRA, which first squeezes the frozen base with quantization so even a 70B model fits on one card.
  • Other members include classic bottleneck adapters, prompt-tuning, and prefix-tuning.
Full fine-tuningPEFT
Trains 100% of parametersTrains ~0.1-1%
Needs 60GB+ VRAM for a 7B modelFits a 7B on a 12-16GB GPU
Output is a whole new multi-GB modelOutput is a few-MB adapter file
Slow, server-grade hardwareFast, runs on one consumer GPU

The library, in code

The beauty of the peft library is that it wraps any standard Hugging Face model the same way, whichever method you pick. You describe the method in a config, call get_peft_model, and train as normal:

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B")
cfg = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules="all-linear")
model = get_peft_model(base, cfg)   # base frozen, adapter attached
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 8.9M || all params: 1.7B || trainable%: 0.52

That last printout is the whole point: you train half a percent of the model. When you are done, you do not save a giant model — you save the small add-on, called an adapter:

model.save_pretrained("my-adapter")  # a few MB on disk, not GB

Later you reload the original base and attach the adapter on top. You can keep a drawer of adapters and hot-swap them — switching which one is active without reloading the heavy base.

Where PEFT fits in the toolchain

You rarely call peft entirely alone. Training frameworks like the TRL library, Axolotl, and Unsloth all sit on top of peft and hand it the config, so a fine-tune becomes a short YAML file rather than a research project. PEFT is the engine; those are the dashboards.

One thing to keep straight: PEFT changes the model's behaviour and style cheaply, but a tiny adapter cannot pour in a whole new field of facts — for fresh knowledge, reach for RAG or continued pretraining instead.

💡 Mental shortcut: "PEFT" the idea = freeze big, train small. "peft" the library = the Python package that does it for any Hugging Face model in three lines.
mentalModel: PEFT is a colour-grade layer on a finished movie: the original footage (the giant model) is frozen and untouched, and you train only a thin, removable edit-layer on top. The Hugging Face library is just the editing software that bolts that layer on in three lines of Python.
takeaways: PEFT = freeze the giant model, train a tiny add-on instead.
Outputs a few-MB adapter, not a whole new multi-GB model.
Great for tone and behaviour; not for cramming in new facts.
mistakes: Confusing the idea with the library. "PEFT" can mean the general approach (freeze big, train small) or the specific Hugging Face peft package. LoRA is a PEFT method; the peft package is one tool that implements it. Be clear which you mean.
Thinking PEFT edits the base model. It does not. The base stays frozen and the learning lives in a separate adapter file. Lose the matching base model and the adapter is useless on its own.

Expecting an adapter to teach big new knowledge. A fraction-of-a-percent update is great at tone, format, and behaviour, but it cannot cram in a whole new domain of facts. For that, use RAG or continued pretraining.

Loading an adapter onto the wrong base. An adapter is shaped to fit one exact base model, version, and chat template. Attach it to a different base (or a differently quantized one) and you get gibberish or a shape-mismatch crash.

Forgetting to merge before high-throughput serving. Running an unmerged LoRA adds a little extra compute per token. If you serve one fixed style at scale, call merge_and_unload() to fold the adapter into the weights for zero overhead — but only if you no longer need to hot-swap.

Saving the wrong thing. Beginners sometimes save the full wrapped model and wonder why it is gigabytes. Use model.save_pretrained() on the PEFT model to write just the tiny adapter, not the frozen base.
exercise:
Install the Hugging Face peft library and wrap a small open model (a 1B-3B open-source Qwen or Llama works on a laptop or free cloud GPU) with a LoraConfig. Call print_trainable_parameters() and write down the trainable percentage. Then run one short training loop on ~30 example sentences teaching a single narrow behaviour (say, always answering in cheerful, jargon-free plain English), save_pretrained() the adapter, and compare the saved folder's size on disk to the full base model's size.

Stretch goal: Train a second adapter for a different style (e.g. a terse, formal tone) on the same frozen base. Then load the base once, register both adapters, and switch between them by name with set_adapter() on the same prompt — proving you can keep one heavy model in memory and flip personalities by activating a different few-MB file.
solution:
Hint: The flow is get_peft_model(base, LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules="all-linear")), then train as usual (the TRL library's SFTTrainer makes the loop trivial). print_trainable_parameters() should report well under 1% trainable — that is the whole win. model.save_pretrained("my-adapter") writes only the small A and B matrices plus a config, so the folder is a few MB versus gigabytes for the base.

For the stretch goal: model.load_adapter(path, adapter_name="formal") registers a second adapter, and model.set_adapter("formal") activates it. The frozen base stays loaded once; only the tiny adapter weights swap, so flipping styles is near-instant.
summary: PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) means freezing the giant model and training only a tiny slice of new numbers — typically 0.1-1% — so a fine-tune fits on one consumer GPU and outputs a few-MB adapter instead of a whole new model. It is both a family of methods (LoRA, QLoRA, bottleneck adapters, prompt-tuning) and a Hugging Face library that bolts any of them onto a standard model in a few lines. Great for tone and behaviour; not for injecting big new knowledge.

diagram.caption: One frozen base model gains a tiny trainable adapter; you save the few-MB adapter and hot-swap different ones for different behaviours.

quiz.q: What does PEFT actually change during training?
options: Every parameter in the base model
Only a small set of new add-on parameters, leaving the base frozen
The training data, not the model
quiz.explain: PEFT freezes the giant base model and trains only a tiny slice (often 0.1-1%) of new parameters. That is what makes it fit on a single consumer GPU.

quiz.q: After a PEFT fine-tune, what do you save and ship to a teammate?
options: Just the few-MB adapter (they reload the matching base themselves)
A merged copy of the base plus optimiser state
quiz.explain: The output is a small adapter, a few MB on disk. It is useless without the exact matching base model, which the teammate loads separately and attaches the adapter to.

quiz.q: A startup wants its model to know thousands of new internal product facts. Is a single small LoRA adapter the right tool?
options: Yes — adapters are designed to memorise large fact sets
No — use RAG or continued pretraining; adapters tune behaviour, not bulk knowledge
Yes, but only if you raise lora_alpha very high
No — fine-tuning of any kind can never change a model
quiz.explain: A fraction-of-a-percent update excels at tone, format, and behaviour but cannot cram in a whole new domain of facts. For fresh knowledge, reach for RAG or continued pretraining.

quiz.q: You serve one fixed adapter at very high throughput and never need to switch styles. What optimisation helps?
options: Call merge_and_unload() to fold the adapter into the base weights
Load three extra adapters for redundancy
Re-quantize the adapter to 4-bit at request time
Train the base model fully instead
quiz.explain: An unmerged LoRA adds a little compute per token. Merging folds the learned change into the weights for zero overhead — but you give up easy hot-swapping, which is fine when the style is fixed.

keyTerms.def: Both an approach and a Hugging Face library: freeze the full model and train only a small set of new parameters instead of all of them.
The older approach of updating every parameter in the model. Accurate but memory-hungry and slow, producing a whole new multi-gigabyte model.
Parameters that are locked and never updated during training. In PEFT the entire base model is frozen; only the small add-on learns.
The small trained add-on that holds the new behaviour. Saved as a tiny file (often a few MB) separate from the base model.
Switching which adapter is active at request time while keeping one frozen base loaded in memory, so one GPU can serve many tasks or styles.
Folding a LoRA adapter's learned change directly into the base weights, giving one fixed model with no extra inference cost (you lose easy swapping).
The PEFT config setting that picks which layers of the model get the trainable add-on. "all-linear" is a common, broad default.
The fast memory on a GPU. The main bottleneck in fine-tuning; PEFT's whole purpose is to slash how much of it a training run needs.
The extra bookkeeping numbers a trainer keeps for every trainable parameter. PEFT trains so few parameters that this bookkeeping nearly vanishes.
